questions.question: What was the main objective of the British in introducing new land revenue systems in India?

options.A: To ensure a steady flow of revenue for the colonial government
options.B: To improve the living conditions of peasants

questions.explanation: The British introduced land revenue systems mainly to secure stable income for administration and expansion.

questions.question: Under the Permanent Settlement of 1793, what responsibility did zamindars have?

options.A: Collect land revenue from peasants and pay it to the British government

questions.explanation: Zamindars acted as intermediaries responsible for collecting land revenue.

questions.question: Why did many traditional zamindars lose their estates during British rule?

options.A: They could not pay the fixed revenue demanded by the British

questions.explanation: Failure to pay revenue led to the auction of their lands.

questions.question: What was one major consequence of the auctioning of zamindari lands?

options.A: New landlords such as merchants and moneylenders emerged

questions.explanation: Merchants and moneylenders often purchased auctioned estates.

questions.question: Why did peasants often fall into a cycle of debt under colonial rule?

options.A: High land revenue demands and loans from moneylenders

questions.explanation: Heavy taxes and dependence on moneylenders created debt traps.

questions.question: What type of crop was indigo considered in colonial agriculture?

options.A: Cash crop

questions.explanation: Indigo was grown mainly for export and profit.

questions.question: Why did European planters encourage the cultivation of indigo in India?

options.A: There was high demand for blue dye in Europe
options.B: Indigo was used as a staple food

questions.explanation: Indigo dye was widely used in European textile industries.

questions.question: What system forced peasants to grow indigo using advance payments?

options.A: The Nij system

questions.explanation: Under the Nij system, planters controlled production and forced peasants to grow indigo.

questions.question: Why did peasants resent the cultivation of indigo?

options.A: It exhausted the soil and reduced food crop production

questions.explanation: Indigo cultivation reduced soil fertility and food crop availability.

questions.question: Which event marked the organized resistance of peasants against indigo cultivation?

options.A: Indigo Revolt

questions.explanation: The Indigo Revolt (1859–60) was a peasant uprising against indigo planters.

questions.question: What strategy did peasants use during the Indigo Revolt?

options.A: They refused to grow indigo

questions.explanation: Refusal to cultivate indigo was the main form of protest.

questions.question: Which government body investigated the grievances of peasants after the Indigo Revolt?

options.A: Indigo Commission

questions.explanation: The Indigo Commission was established to investigate the revolt.

questions.question: Why did many peasants prefer cultivating food crops rather than indigo?

options.A: Food crops were essential for survival
options.D: Food crops had no economic value

questions.explanation: Peasants relied on food crops for their livelihood.

questions.question: What role did moneylenders play in rural colonial society?

options.A: Provided loans to peasants at high interest

questions.explanation: Moneylenders became important in rural credit systems.

questions.question: Which region became the centre of indigo cultivation in India?

options.A: Bengal

questions.explanation: Bengal was the main region for indigo plantations.

questions.question: Why did European planters rely on advances to control peasants?

options.A: Advances forced peasants to grow indigo to repay loans

questions.explanation: Loans tied peasants to indigo cultivation.

questions.question: What was one major effect of colonial revenue policies on rural society?

options.A: Increased rural poverty and debt

questions.explanation: High taxes and exploitation increased poverty.

questions.question: Why did the British encourage the cultivation of cash crops like indigo?

options.A: To increase profits from exports

questions.explanation: Cash crops generated high export profits.

questions.question: Which group benefited most from indigo cultivation?

options.A: European planters

questions.explanation: European planters made large profits from indigo exports.

questions.question: What does the Indigo Revolt demonstrate about colonial rural society?

options.A: Peasants could organize resistance against exploitation

questions.explanation: The revolt showed collective peasant resistance.

questions.question: Why did peasants depend heavily on moneylenders?

options.A: To pay land revenue and meet daily expenses

questions.explanation: Heavy taxes forced peasants to borrow money.

questions.question: Which factor contributed to the decline of traditional zamindars?

options.A: Rigid revenue demands

questions.explanation: Rigid revenue demands forced many zamindars into bankruptcy.

questions.question: Why was indigo cultivation harmful to peasants’ livelihoods?

options.A: It reduced food crop production and soil fertility

questions.explanation: Indigo cultivation reduced land for food crops.

questions.question: What was one major outcome of the Indigo Revolt?

options.A: The Indigo Commission investigated planter practices
options.C: Peasants were forced to grow more indigo

questions.explanation: The British government set up the Indigo Commission.

questions.question: Which aspect best explains the relationship between planters and peasants?

options.A: Economic exploitation through forced cultivation

questions.explanation: Planters exploited peasants by forcing them to grow indigo.
